sections.heading: AI vs ML
sections.body: AI is the broad goal of building systems that behave intelligently; machine learning is the dominant modern approach to reaching that goal — instead of hand-coding every rule, an ML system learns patterns directly from data. Every ML system is a form of AI, but not every AI technique (like classical search or logic-based planning) involves learning from data.

sections.heading: Learning Types
sections.body: Machine learning is commonly split into three broad types: supervised learning (learning from labeled examples), unsupervised learning (finding structure in unlabeled data), and reinforcement learning (learning through trial-and-error interaction with an environment, guided by rewards).

sections.heading: Dataset
sections.body: A dataset is the collection of examples an ML system learns from — typically split into training data (used to fit the model) and testing data (held back to evaluate how well the model generalizes to examples it hasn't seen before).

sections.heading: Features
sections.body: Features are the measurable input variables describing each example in a dataset — for instance, a house-price model might use square footage, number of bedrooms, and location as features that the model uses to make predictions.

sections.heading: Labels
sections.body: Labels are the known correct answers associated with training examples in supervised learning — for example, the actual sale price of each house in a price-prediction dataset. Unsupervised learning, by contrast, works with data that has no labels at all.

sections.heading: Model
sections.body: A model is the learned mathematical representation that maps inputs (features) to outputs (predictions) — it starts with adjustable internal parameters and is shaped by the training process to fit patterns in the data.

sections.heading: Training
sections.body: Training is the process of adjusting a model's parameters using the training dataset, typically by iteratively reducing the difference between the model's predictions and the actual known labels (in supervised learning).

sections.heading: Testing
sections.body: Testing evaluates a trained model's performance on data it was never trained on — this is essential to check whether the model has actually learned generalizable patterns, rather than just memorizing the specific training examples it saw.

quiz.question: How does ML relate to AI?
options: ML is a dominant modern approach to achieving AI's broader goal, learning from data instead of hand-coded rules
AI is a subset of ML
ML replaces the need for any data

quiz.question: Why is a model evaluated on a separate test set rather than the training set?
options: It doesn't matter which set is used
To check whether it generalizes to new data, rather than just memorizing training examples
Training data is never available for evaluation

quiz.question: In supervised learning, labels represent:
options: The known correct answers associated with training examples
